challenges.title: Print a greeting
challenges.description: Print the text 'Hello, World!' to the console.
challenges.hint: Use the built-in print() function with a string argument.

steps.title: Understand the task
steps.explanation: You need to display text in the console. Python's print() function writes any value to standard output.

steps.title: Write the solution
steps.explanation: Call print() with the string 'Hello, World!' as its argument.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: Run the script. You should see 'Hello, World!' printed in the console.

challenges.title: Sum of a list
challenges.description: Write a function `list_sum(numbers)` that returns the sum of all integers in a list.
challenges.hint: Python has a built-in sum() function, or you can loop over the list.

steps.title: Understand the task
steps.explanation: You need to add up every number in the list and return the total.

steps.title: Use the built-in
steps.explanation: Python's built-in sum() accepts any iterable and returns the total.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: list_sum([1, 2, 3]) should return 6. list_sum([]) should return 0.

challenges.title: FizzBuzz
challenges.description: Write a function `fizzbuzz(n)` that returns a list of strings from 1 to n. For multiples of 3 use 'Fizz', multiples of 5 use 'Buzz', multiples of both use 'FizzBuzz', otherwise use the number as a string.
challenges.hint: Check divisibility with the modulo operator %. Order matters: check both 3 and 5 first.

steps.title: Iterate 1 to n
steps.explanation: Use range(1, n + 1) to iterate over all integers from 1 to n inclusive.

steps.title: Check divisibility in order
steps.explanation: Check 15 (both 3 and 5) first to avoid accidentally returning 'Fizz' or 'Buzz' for FizzBuzz numbers.

steps.title: Handle remaining cases
steps.explanation: Use elif for 3 and 5, and else to stringify the number.

challenges.title: LRU Cache
challenges.description: Implement an LRU (Least Recently Used) cache class with a fixed capacity. It must support O(1) `get(key)` and `put(key, value)` operations. `get` returns -1 if the key is not found. When capacity is exceeded, evict the least recently used entry.
challenges.hint: Combine an OrderedDict (or a dict + doubly-linked list) for O(1) access and O(1) eviction.

steps.title: Choose the data structure
steps.explanation: OrderedDict maintains insertion order and supports move_to_end(), which gives us O(1) reordering.

steps.title: Implement get
steps.explanation: On a cache hit, move the key to the end (most recently used) before returning its value.

steps.title: Implement put
steps.explanation: Insert or update the key, then move it to the end. If over capacity, pop from the front (LRU).
